A curated directory of human-written websites, blogs, and corners of the internet worth visiting

import { Card, CardGrid, Aside } from '@astrojs/starlight/components';
I'm still in the process of building this site, so expect changes and updates as I go.
**If you have a Blog, please link back to this site so others can find it too.**
This is a curated collection of **human-written websites, blogs, and corners of the internet worth visiting**. No AI sludge. No recycled SEO filler. No empty content farms pretending to be useful. Just real people writing real things.
This isn’t trying to be the biggest directory on the internet. It’s trying to be a **good one**.
--- ## What makes it different Every site here has been found manually, checked properly, and added because it actually feels worth your time. This isn’t a giant dump of links. The aim is to keep things tight, useful, and worth browsing. You can browse by theme, mood, and interest — not just scroll through noise and hope something sticks. If something feels lifeless, mass-produced, or machine-written, it doesn’t belong here. --- ## What you’ll find here Blogs with voice, personality, reflection, and lived experience. Local writing, history, landscapes, and life rooted somewhere real. Travel stories with substance, not just affiliate fluff. Thoughtful technical writing, indie developers, and people building things. Small forums, niche communities, and internet spaces that still feel alive. Platforms and tools people use to run their own sites. --- ## Why this exists The modern web is full of things that look useful for about three seconds. AI-written articles with no point of view. Affiliate pages pretending to be recommendations. Copy-and-paste content written to rank instead of say anything. Websites chasing traffic with nothing behind them. This exists because the web is better when you can still stumble across something real. A weird personal blog. A thoughtful essay. A niche site made by someone who clearly cares. A page that feels like a person, not a strategy.
This isn’t a place for AI content, affiliate spam, or content written just to rank.
--- ## How sites get added There’s no automation here. Everything is done by hand. A site gets found, checked, then placed where it actually fits. A short description gets written properly, not generated. That means things move slower. That’s intentional.
If it doesn’t feel human, it doesn’t go on the site.
--- ## Built simply The site is intentionally lightweight. Static, fast, and ideal for a content-first site like this. Keeps everything clean, structured, and easy to browse. Entries are written manually, which keeps things simple and portable. No database. No unnecessary complexity. No tracking nonsense. --- ## Want to suggest a site? If you know something worth adding, send it over. Good fits: Personal blogs Niche websites Independent creators Small projects with real thought behind them Communities that actually feel alive Not a fit: AI content mills Parasite SEO pages Thin affiliate sites Generic “top 10” sludge --- ## Final note This isn’t about building the biggest list. It’s about building one that still feels like it was made by a person. And that matters.

Forums and niche communities worth joining

import { Card, CardGrid } from '@astrojs/starlight/components'; These are places where people still talk properly — not just scroll, like, and disappear. Smaller, focused forums often have better conversations than massive platforms. A curated collection of forums and online communities where real people still interact. No spam, no empty platforms — just active places with actual discussion. --- ### 🇬🇧 UK & General Communities * ISPreview Forums – UK broadband, ISP issues, and telecom discussions. * Overclockers UK Forums – Hardware, gaming, and general chat. * AVForums – Home cinema, audio, and tech discussions. * MoneySavingExpert Forums – UK-focused money advice and community support. --- ### 💻 Tech & Internet Culture * Ars Technica Forums – Deep tech discussions and knowledgeable users. * Lobsters – Hacker News-style community with better moderation and discussion. * Hacker News – Tech, startups, and thoughtful discussion threads. * Level1Techs Forum – Hardware, Linux, and self-hosting discussions. --- ### 🎮 Gaming & Hobby Communities * RLLMUK – Long-running UK gaming forum with strong community. * ResetEra – Large gaming forum with active discussions and news. * Something Awful Forums – Classic internet forum with a unique culture. --- ### 🧠 Discussion, Ideas & Life * Straight Dope Message Board – Questions, debates, and general knowledge. * Mr. Money Mustache Forum – Financial independence and lifestyle discussion. * City-Data Forum – Real-world discussions about places, life, and experiences. --- ### 🌐 Indie & Smaller Communities * IndieWeb Forum – People building and owning their own websites. * Privacy Guides Forum – Privacy, security, and digital independence. * NodeBB Community – Forum software community (good insight into modern forums). --- ### Why forums still matter Forums are one of the last places online where conversations feel real. No algorithm deciding what you see. No endless scrolling. Just people talking, helping, arguing, and sharing knowledge. They might look old school - but that’s exactly why they still work.
Smaller, focused communities built around shared interests and hobbies

This site is built by hand, and it grows through people like you.

If you’ve come across a great blog, community, or personal site, you can help others find it too.

---

## 📬 How to submit a site

The easiest way is through GitHub:

👉 [Submit a site](https://github.com/WesTIChU/blogr.directory/issues/new/choose)

You’ll be asked for a few simple things:

Site name  
URL  
Category  
A short description  
Why it belongs here  

I go through every submission myself before anything gets added.

If you’d rather not use GitHub, you can also send it by email.

Just put together the same details and send it to:

add [at] blogr [dot] directory

---

## ✏️ Improve an existing page

Spotted something off?

A typo, a broken link, or even just a better way to describe something, feel free to fix it.

At the bottom of each page, you’ll find an **“Edit this page on GitHub”** link.

Clicking that will:

- Open the page in GitHub  
- Let you make changes directly in your browser  
- Create a suggested edit (pull request)  

I’ll review it and approve it if it looks good.

You don’t need to be a developer — just make the change and submit it.

---

### 🔍 How links are checked

Every site gets a quick manual check before it’s listed.

I’ll usually:

- Visit the site and make sure it’s working properly  
- Check for recent posts or activity  
- Look for signs that it’s still maintained  

If there aren’t clear dates on the site, I’ll check the RSS feed to see when it was last updated.

This helps keep the directory full of active, genuinely maintained sites.

That said, older or inactive sites can still be included if the content is genuinely worth reading.

---

## ✅ What gets accepted

Personal blogs and websites  
Independent writers  
Small communities and forums  
Thoughtful, original content  
Anything with a clear human voice  

Sites built with modern tools are completely fine too.  
That includes AI helping with layout, design, or structure.

What matters is the writing behind it.

---

## ❌ What won’t be added

Fully AI-written content farms  
Affiliate spam or SEO churn sites  
Generic “top 10” blog networks  
Corporate publishing platforms  
Low-effort or clearly abandoned sites  

---

## 🧠 A few simple guidelines

Keep descriptions short and honest  
Check it’s not already listed  
Make sure the site is active  
Link to the actual site, not just social profiles  

---

## 🤖 AI & tools

I’m not against AI.

If anything, it’s helped more people get online who maybe wouldn’t have had the tools or skills before, and I think that’s a good thing.

If your site uses AI for layout, code, or structure, that’s completely fine.

But the content itself should be human.

Real thoughts.  
Real experiences.  
A real voice behind it.

That’s the line.

---

## 💭 Why this exists

The modern internet is full of noise.

AI content, recycled articles, and platforms designed to keep you scrolling.

I wanted something quieter.

A more human web, where people write, share, and build things that actually mean something.

---

## 🙌 Thanks

Every good submission improves this place.

If you’ve got something worth sharing, send it in.

Platforms and tools used to create and publish blogs

import { Card, CardGrid } from '@astrojs/starlight/components'; Use a hosted platform if you just want to write and publish without setup. Self-hosted tools give you full control over your site, design, and content. Tools and platforms people use to write and publish blogs — from simple hosted options to fully self-hosted setups. --- ### 🧱 Hosted Platforms * WordPress.com – Hosted version of WordPress, easy to get started. * Ghost – Clean, modern publishing platform focused on writing. * Substack – Newsletter-style blogging with built-in audience tools. * Medium – Large publishing platform with built-in readership. * Write.as – Minimal, privacy-focused writing platform. --- ### 🧠 Self-Hosted Platforms * WordPress.org – Full control, plugins, themes, and flexibility. * Ghost (Self-Hosted) – Fast, modern, and focused on content. * Grav – Flat-file CMS, no database required. * Jekyll – Static site generator used with GitHub Pages. * Hugo – Fast static site generator for blogs and sites. --- ### ⚡ Modern & Developer-Friendly * Astro – Static-first framework for content-rich websites. * Next.js – React framework for building fast websites. * Nuxt – Vue-based framework for modern web apps. * SvelteKit – Lightweight framework for fast web apps. --- ### 📝 Minimal & Indie Platforms * Bear Blog – Simple, no-nonsense blogging platform. * Micro.blog – Indie blogging and micro-posting platform. * Blot – Turns a folder into a blog. * Listed – Blogging powered by Standard Notes. --- 👉 If you're unsure: start with WordPress.com or Ghost, then move to self-hosted later if you want more control. ### Why these platforms? These are tools used by real people writing real blogs, not content farms or mass-produced sites. Some are simple, some are powerful, but all of them allow people to publish their own voice on the web.
Hosting services used to run blogs, websites, and online projects

import { Card, CardGrid, Badge } from '@astrojs/starlight/components'; Use beginner hosting if you just want your site online quickly without setup. VPS and cloud hosting give you more control, flexibility, and room to grow. Services that power blogs and websites — from beginner-friendly hosting to full control VPS and modern deployment platforms. --- ### 🚀 Beginner-Friendly Hosting * Bluehost – Popular starter hosting, especially for WordPress. * SiteGround – Reliable hosting with good performance and support. * Hostinger – Affordable hosting with a simple interface. * DreamHost – Long-standing provider with solid features. --- ### ⚙️ Managed Hosting * WP Engine – Premium managed WordPress hosting. * Kinsta – Fast, modern hosting built on Google Cloud. * Flywheel – Simple managed WordPress hosting. --- ### 🧠 VPS & Cloud Servers * Hetzner – Powerful, affordable VPS and dedicated servers. * DigitalOcean – Developer-friendly VPS with simple pricing. * Linode – Reliable cloud hosting with good performance. * Vultr – Flexible VPS with global locations. * RackNerd – Budget VPS deals. * HostHatch – High storage VPS and good value plans. * BuyVM – Cheap VPS with strong community reputation. * GreenCloud VPS – Affordable VPS with good performance options. * Time4VPS – Budget European VPS hosting. * LowEndBox – Deals and listings for cheap VPS providers. --- ### ⚡ Modern & Edge Hosting * Vercel – Ideal for frontend frameworks and static sites. * Netlify – Easy deployment for static and JAMstack sites. * Cloudflare Pages – Fast global hosting with built-in CDN. * Render – Modern platform for web apps and services. --- 👉 If you're unsure: start with Hostinger or SiteGround. If you're more technical, go with Hetzner or DigitalOcean. For tools like Coolify, CapRover, and Dokku, see the Self-Hosting page. ### Why these hosting providers? These are services used by real bloggers and developers to run their sites. Some are beginner-friendly, some give full control, but all of them are widely used and reliable depending on your setup.
Tools and platforms for running your own apps, websites, and services

import { Card, CardGrid, Badge } from '@astrojs/starlight/components'; Self-hosting lets you run your own websites, apps, and services on a VPS or home server. You manage everything — from deployments to updates — but gain flexibility, privacy, and independence. Tools that help you run your own websites, apps, and services — without relying on traditional hosting platforms. --- ### 🧰 Self-Hosting Platforms * Coolify – Self-hosted alternative to Vercel and Heroku with a clean UI. * CapRover – Self-hosted alternative to Heroku with simple app deployments. * Dokku – Lightweight, self-hosted Heroku-style platform. * Portainer – Self-hosted UI for managing Docker containers. * Umbrel – Personal server platform, similar to an app store for self-hosting. * CasaOS – Beginner-friendly home server dashboard, similar to Umbrel. --- ### ⚙️ Containers & Infrastructure * Docker – Runs apps in containers, like lightweight virtual machines. * Kubernetes – Advanced system for managing Docker containers at scale. * LinuxServer.io – Prebuilt Docker containers for common self-hosted apps. --- ### 🔐 Networking & Access * Tailscale – Private network (VPN) that lets you access your server from anywhere. * Nginx Proxy Manager – Easy UI for routing domains to your apps. * Traefik – Advanced reverse proxy for dynamic setups. * Cloudflare – Adds DNS, security, and CDN in front of your server. --- 👉 If you're starting out: use Coolify or CapRover on a VPS like Hetzner. Add Tailscale for remote access and keep things simple. ### Why self-host? Self-hosting gives you full control over your websites and data. It’s more hands-on than traditional hosting - but it lets you build, experiment, and own your corner of the web completely.
Tools to run, manage, and grow your own website or online community

import { Card, CardGrid, Badge } from '@astrojs/starlight/components'; These tools help you power your blog, community, and services without relying on big platforms. From forums to monitoring — everything here can run on your own VPS or server. Tools that connect directly to running a website — from forums and comments to monitoring and analytics. --- ### 💬 Communities & Forums * Discourse – Modern forum software used by many large communities. * Flarum – Lightweight and clean forum platform. * NodeBB – Real-time forum powered by Node.js. --- ### 💭 Comments & Interaction * Isso – Self-hosted alternative to Disqus. * Utterances – GitHub-based comment system. * Giscus – Modern comment system powered by GitHub Discussions. --- ### 📊 Analytics (Privacy-Friendly) * Plausible – Simple, privacy-focused analytics. * Umami – Lightweight alternative to Google Analytics. * Matomo – Full-featured analytics platform. --- ### 📡 Monitoring & Uptime * Uptime Kuma – Monitor your website uptime with a clean dashboard. * Healthchecks – Monitor cron jobs and background tasks. * Cachet – Status page system for your services. --- ### 📬 Newsletters & Email * Listmonk – Self-hosted newsletter and mailing list manager. * Mailtrain – Email marketing platform you host yourself. --- ### ⚙️ Automation & Integrations * n8n – Automate workflows between your apps and services. * Activepieces – Open-source automation platform. --- 👉 Start simple: add Umami for analytics and Uptime Kuma to monitor your site. Add forums or comments later as your site grows. ### Why these tools? These tools help you go beyond just publishing a website — they let you build a real, independent online presence. From communities to analytics, you control everything instead of relying on third-party platforms.
